hypothesis: Adding a 'strong-showdown-caller' pattern to the memory model should reduce tail-risk losses when the villain calls multiple streets. In test-2c we observed that the villain won 82-91% of showdowns (significant variance, but consistent), and the agent had no pattern encoding 'this opponent's calls are value-heavy — do not bluff into them at showdown.' The expected effect is a reduction in large losing hands (>50 chips) driven by missed bluffs at showdown, with a corresponding improvement in chips/hand. Control sessions are test-2c 100-hand runs; treatment sessions run 200 hands to give the new pattern sufficient time to accumulate evidence and influence late-session play. Per-session counts are not comparable across groups — use per-hand rates only.
